resume:
Daniel Okafor — Commercial Contracts Attorney (admitted NY 2017)

Senior Counsel, Commercial — Meridian Logistics (2021–present)
- Sole counsel for the procurement and vendor org; ~120 contracts/year.
- Built the company's first contract playbook and clause library.

Associate — Hale & Brandt LLP (2017–2021)
- Commercial transactions group; SaaS, MSAs, data-processing addenda.

Education: J.D., Fordham Law (2017); B.A. Economics, Rutgers (2014)
Licenses: New York State Bar
Focus: SaaS agreements, DPAs, limitation-of-liability, indemnification, SLAs
jd: Commercial Counsel (mid-level, 4–7 yrs) for a Series C B2B SaaS company. You will own the full vendor and customer contract lifecycle: drafting and negotiating MSAs, order forms, DPAs, and security addenda; advising sales on redlines under deal-cycle pressure; and maintaining a self-serve playbook so reps can close standard deals without legal review. You will partner with security and privacy on GDPR/CCPA obligations and own the limitation-of-liability and indemnity positions in our paper. We need someone who can articulate, clause by clause, where the company will and won't move, and why — and who can quantify the commercial exposure behind each position. Reports to the GC. Hands-on drafting role, not a manager.

history.q: Walk me through your current scope at Meridian — what kind of agreements, what volume?
You built their first contract playbook. What problem was it solving?
Give me a concrete clause where your playbook sets a fallback position. Which one?
On limitation of liability — what's your default cap, and what's your first fallback?
Why 12 months of fees as the cap rather than, say, a fixed dollar amount?
Where do you carve out from that cap? What sits in the super-cap or uncapped bucket?
A vendor demands mutual uncapped liability for data breach. How do you respond?
Walk me through a specific negotiation where you had to hold a position under deal pressure.
Sales wanted to close by quarter-end. How did you decide what to concede?
What was the commercial exposure if you'd just given them the uncapped indemnity?
How did your playbook change after that deal, if at all?
Quantify it for me: on that breach-indemnity negotiation, what was the actual dollar exposure you were protecting against, and how did you size it?

candidate_last_answer: Concretely: the customer was a healthcare network processing roughly 400,000 patient records through our platform. I sized the exposure two ways. First, statutory — under the breach framework that applies, regulated records carry a per-record cost in the low-hundreds of dollars once you blend notification, credit monitoring, and regulatory penalties, so 400K records put the realistic tail around $40–80 million. Second, the contract value was $1.1M annual. An uncapped data-breach indemnity meant we'd be accepting a roughly 40-to-70x exposure on a deal earning us about $1.1M a year. So I held a super-cap at 3x annual fees for the breach carve-out, conceded a faster notification SLA instead, and we closed two days before quarter-end.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: a per-record cost in the low-hundreds of dollars once you blend notification, credit monitoring, and regulatory penalties

expected-yield: a defensible breakdown of the per-record cost estimate — the source of the low-hundreds figure and how notification, credit monitoring, and penalty components were each weighted — testing whether the candidate's sharp number is grounded in a real methodology or a confident guess
